Instead, there are gardens—actual gardens growing in actual soil, not in hydroponic towers but in the ground itself. Paths wind between them, and children run across them. People sit on benches that were built by someone’s hands rather than printed by a machine. The buildings are not the uniform cubes of the Merit Zone, identical on the outside with variation hidden within. They are varied, eccentric, personal: some made of wood, some of stone, some of recycled materials that once served other purposes, some with murals painted by residents, some with additions that clearly came later as families grew or ideas changed. There is a smell of bread baking somewhere, and woodsmoke, and the particular sweetness of ripening fruit you cannot identify but want to immediately. This is not a designed aesthetic; it is the physical residue of thousands of individual decisions made over years by people who were free to build as they wished, constrained only by what their neighbors would tolerate and what their own hands could manage.


Values Without a Written Code
The Cooperative Commonwealth grows organically from the decisions of the people who live there, and because those people have chosen this zone rather than any other, the things they build and the ways they organize themselves tend to reflect a common set of values, even though no one has written them down. They value shared ownership over private accumulation. They value participation over delegation. They value the process of deciding together even when it is slow and messy, because they believe that time spent in deliberation is not wasted but invested in the relationships that make collective life possible. They value work that is meaningful and visible—work that connects them to each other and to the physical world, work that produces things they can see and touch and share. They value stability and continuity, not because they fear change but because they believe that trust takes time to build and that institutions which endure across generations accumulate wisdom that no single generation can generate on its own.


Governance: Voice, Deliberation, and the Community Assembly
The governance of the Cooperative Commonwealth is not a single system but a family of systems, all rooted in the principle that those who are affected by a decision should have a voice in making it. At the most local level, a neighborhood of fifty or a hundred families gathers regularly in whatever space is available—a community hall, a cleared garden, someone’s expanded living room—to discuss the things that concern them directly: the maintenance of shared paths, the organization of children’s activities, the resolution of disputes between neighbors, and the planning of communal meals and celebrations. These gatherings have no formal rules beyond what the participants agree to in the moment. Sometimes they use consensus because they believe a decision everyone can live with is better than one imposed by a majority. Sometimes they use majority vote because consensus is too slow and the matter is urgent. Sometimes they simply talk until everyone is tired and someone says “let’s try it this way and see how it goes,” and no one objects, and that becomes the decision by default. To an observer from the Merit Zone, with its precise metrics and transparent protocols, this informality would look like chaos, but to the residents it is simply how people who know and trust each other get things done.


Above the neighborhood level, there are councils and assemblies that coordinate across larger scales. A district council might bring together representatives from each neighborhood to discuss shared infrastructure, the allocation of resources coming from the zone’s participation in the wider Federation, and the planning of zone-wide events. These representatives are not elected in the usual sense. They are usually selected by lot from among those who volunteer, or they rotate through the position so that everyone serves eventually, or they are chosen by acclamation because everyone knows who has the time and skill to do the work well. The councils have no power to compel anyone to do anything. They can only recommend, coordinate, and ask. If a neighborhood decides not to participate in a zone-wide project, that is their right. But over time, norms develop and expectations solidify, and most people find that participating in the larger coordination makes their own lives easier and richer, so they go along voluntarily.


The most visible institution is the Community Assembly, held once a month on the first Sunday in the central plaza—a large open space surrounded by gardens and workshops and buildings that have grown over time. The Assembly has no fixed agenda. Anyone who wants to bring something before the community stands up and speaks. Someone might propose applying for Federation funding to build a new workshop. Someone else might raise a concern about a neighbor who has been struggling. A group of young people might present a plan for a new playground. A council might report on its activities and ask for guidance. There is no time limit on speeches, though people who go on too long will eventually hear murmuring and someone will gently suggest they wrap up. There is no parliamentary procedure, no Robert’s Rules, no formal motions and amendments. People talk until they have said what they need to say, and then someone asks, “What should we do?” and the conversation shifts toward finding a path forward. When a decision is needed, the facilitator—always someone trusted to be fair—asks if there is consensus, looking around the circle to see if anyone strongly objects. If no one does, the decision is made. If someone objects, the conversation continues until either the objection is addressed or it becomes clear that consensus is impossible. In that case, a show of hands may gauge the strength of feeling. If the objection is from a small minority, the majority might proceed anyway, but knowing they are imposing on their neighbors, they will try to accommodate those who disagree. If the objection is widespread, the proposal is set aside. The goal is never to win but to find a way forward that as many people as possible can live with.


This process is deliberately slow. Visitors from the Merit Zone, accustomed to decisions made in minutes by AI-optimized protocols, find it agonizing. Visitors from the Liquid Republic, who can delegate their vote to experts and forget about most issues, find it exhausting. Visitors from the Anarchist Federation, who believe that collective decision-making is inherently coercive, find it baffling. But the residents have chosen this slowness. They believe that decisions made in haste are decisions made poorly, that the time spent in deliberation is not wasted but invested in relationships, and that a decision everyone can live with, even if it takes weeks, is better than one imposed by a majority in an afternoon. They have learned, through years of practice, that working through disagreement together builds trust and understanding that no efficient mechanism can replicate.


Economy: Cooperatives, Reciprocity, and the Informal Web
The economy of the Cooperative Commonwealth is what makes it most distinctive. The Federation’s credit circulates everywhere, and people need it to trade with other zones and to pay the Land Value Tax that funds the Commons dividend. But within the zone, most exchange happens through mechanisms that are not quite market and not quite gift.


Worker cooperatives own and operate businesses together. A bakery, for instance, might be owned by the five people who bake there. When you buy bread, you pay in credits, but the price is set to cover costs and provide a decent living, not to maximize profit. Any surplus is invested in better equipment, shared among the members, or used to subsidize bread for neighbors who are struggling. Consumer cooperatives allow people to pool their purchasing power to buy food in bulk from the bio-domes or to contract with manufacturers in other zones for goods they need. Housing cooperatives let residents collectively own their buildings and decide together how to maintain them and who gets to live in them. Credit unions let people save together and lend to each other at rates they set themselves.


Woven through all of this is a dense network of mutual aid and informal exchange that never touches the formal economy at all. People trade skills, labor, and goods with their neighbors, keeping mental accounts of who owes what to whom, settling balances over time through continued reciprocity. If you help someone build a shed, they might help you harvest your garden. If you watch their children for an afternoon, they might bring you soup when you are sick. None of this is tracked or measured or optimized. It is simply what people do when they live together and trust each other.


The Economy of Care
Perhaps the most distinctive feature of the Cooperative Commonwealth is the way it treats care. In the Merit Zone, care work is measured and scored like everything else—hours with elders counted toward social contribution, childcare tracked and verified. In the Anarchist Federation, care is a private matter arranged through market exchange or family obligation. In the Liquid Republic, it is a policy domain subject to delegation and expert opinion. But here, care is understood as the foundation of everything else, the invisible work without which no society can function. It is organized as a collective responsibility rather than an individual burden.


There are childcare cooperatives where parents share the work of raising children, rotating through shifts so that no one is overwhelmed and every child has many adults who know and love them. There are elder care collectives where older residents live together, supported by younger neighbors who visit regularly and help with what needs doing. There are health mutuals where people pool resources to cover medical needs that the universal basics do not fully address, and where the work of caring for the sick is shared rather than left to professionals alone. There are mental health circles where people gather regularly to talk about what they are struggling with, to listen to each other, and to offer the kind of support that no therapist can provide, because it comes from people who know your life and your context. All of this work is valued not through credits or multipliers or status badges but through the simple recognition that it matters—that the people who do it are essential, that without them the whole thing would fall apart. The recognition takes the form of gratitude, of reciprocity, of the knowledge that when you need care, others will be there for you because you were there for them.


Education: Learning Through Living
The education of children in the Cooperative Commonwealth reflects the same values. There are no self-discovery pods, no AI-optimized learning paths, no merit-tracked progress. Instead, children learn alongside adults in the daily work of the community. A child who wants to learn baking spends time in the bakery, helping, watching, gradually taking on more responsibility until they can bake a loaf themselves. A child curious about building follows the carpenters and stonemasons, learns to handle tools, to measure and cut and join, to see a project through from start to finish. A child who loves stories sits with the elders and listens, then tells stories of their own, and learns that the tradition continues through them. There is no curriculum, no schedule, no testing. There is only immersion in a world of meaningful work and the freedom to find your place within it. The adults trust that children will learn what they need to learn when they need to learn it, and that the things they learn by doing will stay with them in ways that abstract lessons never can.


Relationship to the Wider Federation
The Cooperative Commonwealth is deeply integrated with the rest of the Federation, but it remains fully autonomous. Economically, its cooperatives sell goods to other zones—bread, pottery, furniture, and workshop products. Its residents work in other zones when that makes sense, bringing home credits that circulate through the local economy. It imports what it cannot produce itself: electronics, medical supplies, specialized tools. The balance of trade is tracked through normal Federation mechanisms, and the zone pays its share of the Land Value Tax like everyone else. Culturally, people from the Merit Zone visit for the festivals, marvel at the gardens, eat the bread, and sometimes decide to stay. People from the Commonwealth visit the Merit Zone to see the innovations, to learn about new technologies, to participate in Innovation Day, and occasionally decide that the pace of life there suits them better. The boundaries between zones are porous; people move back and forth over their lives as their needs and desires change.


Politically, the Commonwealth participates in Federation governance. Its residents serve on cross-zone juries and councils, its representatives speak in Federation assemblies, and its disputes with other zones are resolved through the same dispute resolution system that applies everywhere. But within the zone, governance remains autonomous and self-determined. The Federation has no authority to tell the Cooperative Commonwealth how to organize its internal affairs, just as the Cooperative Commonwealth has no authority to tell the Merit Zone how to run its merit system. This mutual autonomy is the foundation of the entire Pluralistic Federation.


Tensions and the Unwritten Constitution
The tensions within the Cooperative Commonwealth are real and persistent, as tensions are in any human community. There is tension between those who want to grow and welcome newcomers and those who fear that growth will dilute the culture. There is tension between those who want to engage more deeply with other zones—trade more, learn more, influence more—and those who want to turn inward and protect what they have built. There is tension between those who thrive in the slow, consensus-based decision-making and those who find it frustrating and want faster processes. There is tension between those who contribute a lot and those who contribute a little, and the question of how to recognize the first without shaming the second. There is tension between the ideal of equality and the reality of differential talent, effort, and need. There is tension between the old who remember why the zone was founded and the young who inherited it and may not feel the same fierce commitment.


Residents do not pretend these tensions do not exist. They talk about them openly, in neighborhood gatherings, monthly Assemblies, and countless informal conversations. They have developed practices for holding them—ways of ensuring that the voices of those who want to grow are heard alongside those who want to stay small, ways of welcoming newcomers without eroding what made the community worth joining, ways of recognizing contribution without creating hierarchy, and ways of passing on the founding vision to each new generation without forcing them to repeat the past. These practices are not written down in any constitution. They are carried in the memories of the old and demonstrated in the actions of the young. They evolve over time as circumstances change. They are the living culture of the zone, the thing that makes it what it is.
Outpost (AUTPOST)
The Outpost (AUTPOST): Autonomous Unmonitored Territory for Peaceful Off‑Grid Subsistence and Trade
(The acronym is deliberately over‑engineered. The only thing less bureaucratic than the Outpost is its name.)


Arriving at the Checkpoint
You can reach the Outpost either by walking the path from District Seven or by taking a tube to the last station at the edge of the city. The tube stops at a small, unstaffed checkpoint – a single building with a desk and a screen. There are no cameras beyond this point. You step out of the tube, walk twenty metres, and you are at the gate. A screen displays a simple message: “You are about to leave the monitored territory. No metrics, no surveillance, no governance beyond what you create. Your consent ends here.”


At the checkpoint, you register your departure from the system. The machine records that you have left – not your name, only that a citizen ID has been deactivated. You are now out of all ledgers, dashboards, and civic records. The attendant is a paid worker for the Commons Trust (rotating shifts, no decision‑making power). They offer you a physical ID card loaded with a small monthly dividend (e.g., 200 credits – enough for basic necessities but not a full living). The card contains an encrypted offline chip and a biometric identifier (thumbprint or iris). No name, no central database. You may refuse the card; barter and aggregate shipments will still be available. If you refuse, you can still request a card later from inside the Outpost – a simple request to the coordinator or at the government shop will issue one on the spot (same offline process, no records kept).


You also receive a single sheet of paper – printed, not digital – that explains everything you need to know about living in the Outpost: the work available (gardening, building, waste processing, tool repair, etc.), how the ID card works, how to make peer‑to‑peer transfers, the weekly packages from the main city, the government shop at the gate, the rules (the single‑page constitution), the bingo‑cage council, the monthly assembly, and the right to leave at any time. The paper is yours to keep. No further instructions are given.


From the checkpoint, you walk twenty minutes through a band of trees. The path is unpaved. After a while, the simulated sky of the bio‑dome gives way to real weather, real mud, and real silence. Then the valley opens before you. No cameras, no sensors, no checkpoints. If you arrive, someone will see you and say, “You are here now.” You say yes. That is the end of procedure.


Daily Life and Work
Buildings are made by hand from wood, stone, and recycled materials. Gardens are dug in actual dirt with actual worms. A stream provides water. There are no supervisors, no RFID logs, no merit points, no demurrage. Work happens because it needs to be done: someone sorts the weekly waste shipment because it piles up; someone builds chairs and leaves them in a common area; someone trades vegetables for bread baked by a neighbour. Trust accumulates over years of living together.


The Dividend Card and Government Shop
The physical ID card gives you a small monthly dividend. If you did not take one at the checkpoint, you can request it at any time from the government shop or from the Outpost coordinator (a Commons Trust worker who handles the weekly shipment – paid, no authority beyond logistics). The card is issued on the spot using an offline terminal; no name, no permanent record.


At the edge of the valley, a small government shop sells only basic goods: staple foods, seeds, simple tools, hygiene products, basic medicines, and raw materials. All purchases use the card via an offline reader. The shop has generous purchase limits (e.g., one bag of rice per week, one tool per month) enforced by the card’s chip. These allow stocking up for months but prevent clearing the inventory. A standalone kiosk (screen, biometric pad, no network) lets you check your balance.


Portable Transfer Devices
You can buy a tiny, hand‑crank or solar‑powered device from the shop. It has two card slots, a biometric pad, and an e‑paper screen. No logs, no network. Two residents meet anywhere – in a garden, by the stream, inside a shelter – insert their cards, authenticate, and transfer any number of credits between them. This creates a local, untracked economy: credits can buy tomatoes, pay for a roof repair, give a gift, or save for a larger purchase. For those without a personal device, a few central machines are fixed at key locations (the shop, the bingo‑cage gathering area). The devices have no balance cap; residents can save for months or years. The only limit is the weekly purchase cap at the shop, which prevents hoarding of physical goods.


Governance: The Bingo‑Cage Council
Every six months, residents gather near the stream. Someone brings a transparent bingo cage with numbered balls. The cage is spun, numbers drop, and the person whose number is called is asked if they want to serve on the council. Seven people say yes. The council has no power to make laws, punish, or compel. It coordinates the weekly shipments (liaising with the Commons Trust worker), welcomes newcomers, and mediates disputes when asked. After six months, they return to ordinary life and cannot serve again for at least one cycle. Once a month, an assembly meets: anyone may speak, propose, and vote by show of hands. The assembly can override the council by two‑thirds and can amend the single sheet of paper posted on a tree – the Outpost’s entire constitution. No one is required to attend anything.


The Weekly Shipment
Once a week, a pallet of goods arrives from the main city: food staples, medicine, seeds, tools, raw materials. In exchange, the Outpost sends back compost, handmade goods, processed materials. Only aggregate weight is tracked, to keep a rough balance. The dividend card and shop are layered on top of this; they do not replace the aggregate shipment, which remains the backstop for those without cards.


Children and the Choice
Children born in the Outpost grow up without metrics. At sixteen (with parental agreement) or eighteen (alone), they may walk up the path to the checkpoint and re‑enter the merit system, starting at 1.0x multiplier with no penalty and no record of where they came from. They may also stay. The choice is real and offered to everyone. The Outpost treats neither leaving nor staying as failure.


Death and Memory
When someone dies, there is a ceremony only if people want one. Sometimes a group buries the body under a tree with a stone. Sometimes no one notices for days. The dead are not recorded in any ledger. Their names never appear on a dashboard. They are simply gone. The stream flows on. This is not seen as a tragedy; it is seen as the way of things.


Why It Exists
The Outpost is proof that Fair Digital Future is not a total system. It is proof that a human being can live from birth to death without ever being counted, optimized, or improved, and that such a life can be good enough for the person living it. It does not solve the problem of meaning – no place can. But it makes space for people who do not want the problem solved. The bench at the edge still asks: Is this enough? For those who live here, the answer is whatever they say it is. That is enough.


Civic Commons Zone
Civic Commons Zone
Definition and Purpose
The Civic Commons is not a separate zone type. It is the Just Living tier located at the physical and administrative gateway of the Pluralistic Federation. It serves three functions: the default zone for anyone who has not made an active choice, the processing center for immigration and emigration, and a permanent home for citizens who prefer a simple, low‑pressure life. Its existence solves the knowledge‑burden problem: you never need to understand the difference between liquid democracy and sortition, demurrage and land value tax, or the Merit Zone’s four‑dimension formula. You arrive, you live, you contribute a small amount of verifiable work, and the system asks nothing more.


Location and Physical Layout
The Civic Commons occupies the district adjacent to the main visitor station and border control point. Every person entering the Federation—immigrant, visitor, or returning citizen—passes through this district first. Every person leaving—by exit package, eviction, or transfer to The Outpost—processes their departure here. The tube line to the rest of the Federation begins at the far edge of the district, but you can live your entire life without ever boarding it.


The district is designed to be unthreatening: wide paths, visible green spaces, open plazas, and a single large building containing the immigration office, the exit processing center, and the orientation hall. Housing pods are clustered near the entrance so newcomers can settle quickly. There are no walls, no checkpoints, no sense of being processed—only a gradual transition from the outside world into the Federation, or from the Federation out again.


Who Lives Here
Three populations reside in the Civic Commons.


New immigrants spend their first three to six months here. They receive universal basics, language training through LinguaGlass, job placement in simple verifiable roles (waste sorting, tube cleaning, farm parcel handling, linen folding), and weekly meetings with a zone coach. The coach is a volunteer citizen serving a six‑month term who answers questions and arranges exploration trips to other zones. After three months, the immigrant may request transfer to any other zone. If they make no request, they remain in the Civic Commons by default. They may stay indefinitely or transfer later.


Permanent residents are citizens who have chosen to remain in the Civic Commons. They may have arrived as immigrants and never left, or they may have transferred from other zones seeking a simpler life. They work the same verifiable minimum jobs (10 hours per week) as immigrants, receive the same universal basics, and have no merit multipliers. They may vote in Federation referenda through auto‑proxy (their vote is delegated to a randomly selected citizen from the same age bracket and district, refreshed annually; they may override any vote or revoke delegation entirely). They may serve on juries if selected, but are never required to. They may leave for another zone at any time by notifying the district office and completing a 30‑day trial period in the destination zone.


Exiting citizens are processed here when they leave the Federation permanently. They receive their exit package (minimum 15,000 credits plus job placement in a partner country) or transfer to The Outpost via the path that begins at the district’s eastern edge. The exit process is dignified, private, and takes less than two hours. No one is shamed or questioned about their reasons.


Governance
The Civic Commons has the simplest governance of any zone. Major decisions (budget, infrastructure, relations with other zones) are handled by the Federation‑level Commons Trust. Local decisions—park maintenance, pod allocation, scheduling of orientation sessions, adding a community garden—are made by a rotating council of five residents selected by sortition every six months. The council has no power to change zone rules; those are fixed by the Federation Core Protocol and the Just Living tier definition.


Any resident may propose a change to local operations. The proposal is voted on by all residents via simple majority, with a 30‑day cooling period. No steel‑manning requirement, no citizen juries, no permanent bureaucracy. Disputes between residents are resolved by the Federation’s standard dispute resolution organizations (default) or by optional local mediation if both parties agree.


The zone has no merit system, no demurrage on local balances (residents use the Federation credit), no local currency, no special housing lottery, no cultural permanence locks, and no defense contribution beyond the Federation minimum. It is intentionally boring. That is its virtue.


Economy and Work
All residents receive the Federation citizen dividend of 2,000 credits per month. No zone top‑up is provided. Work is required at a minimum of 10 hours per week, verified by a simple RFID badge that logs time at task stations. The work menu is fixed and includes only tasks that are simple, verifiable, and necessary for the Federation’s operation: waste sorting, tube pod cleaning, farm parcel handling, linen folding, dishwashing in public cafeterias, component counting, label sticking, and similar. Long‑term residents (over two years with a clean record) may opt into trust‑based verification, where they self‑report hours and are subject to random spot checks. Lying results in one week of double hours.


There is no merit multiplier, no performance tracking beyond hours logged, and no pathway to enhanced stipends or luxury unlocks within the zone. A resident who wants those things must transfer to another zone. The floor is the ceiling, and that is sufficient.


Transition to Other Zones
Any resident may request a 30‑day trial in another zone. For zones without capacity limits (Merit Zone, Liquid Republic, Sortition Republic), acceptance is automatic. For limited‑capacity zones (Cooperative Commonwealth, Anarchist Federation), the resident joins a waiting list and may remain in the Civic Commons until a spot opens.


During the trial, the resident keeps their Civic Commons pod. If they transfer permanently, the pod is released. If they return, they resume the same pod (if still available) or enter the lottery with priority (skipping the 30‑day dormitory requirement). The trial may be extended once by another 30 days if both the resident and the destination zone agree.


Children Growing Up in the Civic Commons
Children born to permanent residents receive universal basics with no work requirement until age 16. From age 12, they participate in structured visits to other zones: one week per school term in a different zone, accompanied by a guide. This ensures they are not isolated from the wider Federation.


At age 16, they must complete a mandatory one‑week orientation on zone differences. They receive a 30‑day trial voucher for any zone of their choice. If they make no choice, they remain in the Civic Commons as permanent residents. They may choose later at any age, with the same trial rights.


Culture and the Bench
Every district in the Federation has a bench facing a wall with the question carved into it: Is this enough? The Civic Commons bench is deliberately placed near the immigration orientation hall, so every newcomer sees it within their first week. There are no pamphlets, no lectures, no required contemplation. The question is simply there.


Beyond the bench, the Civic Commons has minimal cultural infrastructure: one multipurpose room that can be used for meetings, potlucks, film screenings, or children’s activities. No dedicated cultural staff, no Temple, no Museum, no Innovation Day. Residents may attend Federation‑wide festivals by traveling to other zones. Local rituals—weekly potlucks, a monthly “quiet hour” on the bench—emerge organically if residents want them; the council facilitates but does not mandate.


The Outpost Path
At the eastern edge of the district, a simple wooden sign marks the start of a 20‑minute walking path to The Outpost. The sign reads: “To The Outpost – autonomous zone, no tracking, no services beyond this point.”


Any Civic Commons resident may walk the path, stay in The Outpost for up to 72 hours, and return with no paperwork and no penalty. The 72 hours do not count against the weekly work requirement. If a resident wishes to transfer permanently to The Outpost, they notify the council (or simply do not return). This is treated as voluntary exit from the Federation; they receive a reduced exit package of 5,000 credits (because they remain on Federation land but outside the system) and forfeit all Federation basics.


Returning from The Outpost after more than 72 hours but within 30 days requires re‑entering through the immigration process: the resident signs a new 24‑month contract, their previous work history is preserved, but their merit score resets to zero. After 30 days, they are treated as a new immigrant with no priority.


Preventing the Civic Commons from Becoming a Ghetto
The Civic Commons is a choice, not a dumping ground. No one may be transferred here involuntarily as punishment. Citizens evicted from other zones go directly to exit processing, not to the Civic Commons.


Mental health check‑ins are offered annually, voluntarily and confidentially. For residents showing signs of severe distress (reported by peers or council), a check‑in is offered but never forced.


Pathways back to other zones are actively encouraged. Any resident who has lived here for more than one year may request a 30‑day trial in any other zone without losing their pod. The Federation publishes annual statistics on the Civic Commons population: length of stay, reasons for staying, transfer rates to other zones, and satisfaction scores. If the data shows a problem—for example, more than 30% of residents have been here for over five years and have never attempted a trial, or satisfaction scores drop below 70% for two consecutive years—a citizen jury is convened to review conditions and recommend changes.


Monthly social events (zone‑wide meals, work‑swap days, joint path‑cleaning with The Outpost) prevent isolation and stigma. The economic floor is identical to every other zone, so poverty is never a reason to be here.
Zone Options
Zone Options


The Pluralistic Federation is a territory where multiple autonomous zones coexist under a shared minimal foundation called the Core Protocol. Each zone is free to choose its own internal rules, culture, economy, and governance, but all zones share the same physical infrastructure—the tube network, energy grid, water systems, and land trust—and the same basic rights. This document explains everything a citizen needs to know to create a new zone, choose which zone to live in, or change the rules of an existing zone. For every optional module, the possible settings are described alongside their trade-offs, historical precedents where they exist, and the kinds of communities each choice tends to attract.


The Core Protocol: The Unchangeable Foundation


Every zone, without exception, must follow the Core Protocol. These rules cannot be toggled off, waived, or modified by any zone. They are the hard floor beneath all diversity, the minimum that preserves peace, consent, and the shared infrastructure that makes the Federation possible.


The Core Protocol includes the non‑aggression principle: no person or group may initiate force, fraud, or theft against another person or their legitimately owned property. It guarantees freedom of movement between all zones, freedom of association to form any community or contract, and the right to exit with a funded package of at least 15,000 credits plus job placement or transfer to the Outpost. The Commons Trust owns all shared infrastructure—the tubes, reactors, bio‑domes, water systems, and the land itself—and distributes an equal citizen dividend of at least 2,000 credits per month to every registered resident. No zone may reduce or eliminate this dividend, ensuring that poverty is never a weapon.


All disputes must be resolvable through competing, transparent dispute resolution organizations. No zone may establish a monopoly on justice. The Core Protocol also guarantees children’s rights: no permanent body modification without consent, access to basic education, freedom to learn about other zones and ways of life, and no isolation from alternative perspectives. The Whistleblower Sector is embedded in the Core Protocol, guaranteeing that any citizen may report violations safely, anonymously, and without any consequence to their merit, housing, or social standing. Retaliation against a whistleblower is a Core Protocol violation with automatic severe penalties.


Everything else is optional and chosen by each zone through its charter. The following modules describe the choices available.


Governance Modules


Merit system module. Off: everyone receives only the base dividend (maximum equality, no incentive to excel). Basic: 1–3x multipliers based on narrow criteria such as hours worked or tasks completed (simpler, but misses dimensions of contribution like care or creativity). Full: 1–5x multipliers on four dimensions (personal development, social contribution, responsibility, adaptability) with anti‑gaming features such as metric rotation and delayed feedback (default for the Merit Zone). Trade‑off: motivation to contribute vs. complexity of administration vs. status anxiety for those who compare.


Leadership selection. Election (familiar, rewards charisma and campaigning, but creates a political class and periodic disruption). Sortition (lottery from a licensed pool of competent citizens – representative, hard to capture, but may select reluctant leaders). Rotation (every adult serves a short term – prevents entrenchment entirely but forces participation on the unwilling). None (no formal leaders – maximum freedom but may fail to coordinate at scale). Default: sortition for juries and oversight, election for executive councils.


Voting system. Direct (every citizen votes on every issue – pure but time‑intensive and burdensome). Liquid (citizens hold votes on all issues but may delegate them to trusted experts instantly and revoke at any time – balances participation and expertise, the default for the Liquid Republic). Delegative (fixed‑term representatives elected to decide on their behalf – simpler but creates a gap between elections). None (no collective voting – only for small anarchist zones where consensus or voluntary association replaces voting).


Council structure. Unicameral (a single chamber, efficient but with fewer checks on power). Bicameral (two chambers, slower and more deliberative, prevents hasty decisions). Citizen juries only (lightweight, no permanent legislative body – default for small zones and for high‑stakes decisions in larger ones). None (no council; decisions made by direct vote, consensus, or not at all).


Steel‑manning requirement. Off (debate proceeds as usual). On for proposals (proponents must present the strongest opposing argument; default). On for all debate (every speaker must acknowledge the best counter‑argument). Improves decision quality at the cost of time and rhetorical freedom.


Sunset clauses. Off (laws remain until repealed). 10 years (default – most laws expire after a decade unless renewed). 5 years (shorter cycle forces frequent review). Laws that are not actively renewed simply expire.


Metric mortality. Off (performance indicators remain indefinitely). On with 18‑month cycle (default – every KPI category must be reauthorised or it dies, preventing metric gaming).


Economy Modules


Currency. FDF credit only (default – simple, no exchange risk, but no local monetary policy). Credit plus local currency (allows demurrage and local investment incentives but adds complexity). Barter only (pure market without money, scales poorly beyond small communities).


Demurrage (local currency only). 0–15% quarterly decay on idle balances. 0% encourages saving but risks hoarding and stagnation. 2–5% mild circulation incentive. 6–10% strong redistribution, pushing money to flow. 11–15% maximises velocity but can destroy retirement savings. Default: 5% with a 0–10% citizen‑adjustable range.


Property regime. Private property (strong incentives for investment and care, but produces inequality). Usufruct (the right to use and enjoy, but not to sell or permanently alienate; prevents speculation – default for land and housing). Commons (collective ownership with shared management rules – egalitarian but risks the tragedy of the commons if not well governed). Mix (combines private, usufruct, and commons for different asset classes; flexible but complex).


Land value tax. 0–30% on the unimproved value of land. Off (no tax – simple but rewards speculation and idle land). Flat rate (efficient – default 5%). Progressive (higher rates on more valuable land, more redistributive but complex to assess).


Public‑private accelerator. Off (no government co‑investment in innovation). On (citizens vote to fund startups; 70% of profits go to the innovators, 30% recycle into public funds – default). Channels entrepreneurial energy without creating permanent corporate power.


Inheritance. None (default – nothing of material significance passes; merit resets, wealth decays, dynasties are impossible). Limited (only the base housing pod may be passed on). Full (all wealth and status transfer – creates aristocracy, strongly discouraged but available for zones that want it).


Basic stipend top‑up. 0–3,000 extra credits per month on top of the Federation dividend. Default 0. A zone can choose to be more generous, funding the top‑up through its own taxes or resource revenues.


Infrastructure Modules


Tube network access. Full (default – all stations and inter‑zone routes). Limited (only neighbouring zones). None (isolated, must rely on surface travel or virtual connection). A zone that chooses isolation gains quiet but loses easy trade and movement.


Housing type. Modular pods only (efficient, standardised, easy to reconfigure). Mixed (default – pods plus traditional construction). Traditional only (beautiful, culturally expressive, but resource‑intensive). The choice shapes the visual character and flexibility of the zone.


Smart wall integration. Full (walls display information, art, and civic data – default, but with privacy risks). Minimal (basic tablet functions only, walls are static). None (maximum privacy, no digital surfaces; the zone feels pre‑digital).


Energy source. Nuclear plus renewables (default – reliable baseload with green supplement). Renewables only (clean but intermittent; requires storage). Local only (off‑grid, independent but low capacity; suited for small, remote zones).


Food system. Bio‑dome delivery (efficient, year‑round, but disconnects people from food production). Local gardens (community‑tended, labour‑intensive but builds connection to land). Mixed (default – staples from domes, fresh produce from neighbourhood gardens, balancing efficiency and resilience).


Surveillance. None (the Outpost model – maximum privacy, minimal security). Limited (cameras only in tube stations and public plazas, data deleted monthly – default). Full (extensive monitoring of public spaces, but private zones like homes and Havens remain unmonitored).


Culture and Identity Modules


Language policy. Open (any language – default; translation is handled by LinguaGlass and Civic Lingua). Civic Lingua only (the engineered shared tongue for all public life; heritage languages used privately). Heritage language required (preserves a minority culture but may exclude newcomers).


Ceremonies. None (no public rituals). Optional (available but not required – default). Mandatory for certain events (builds shared meaning and cadence to the year but may feel coercive to dissenters).


Architectural style. No restrictions (default – organic, diverse). Traditional codes (preserves a historic character but limits innovation). Uniform modern (egalitarian, efficient, but can feel monotonous).


Calendar. Standard Federation (default – the shared solar calendar with fixed holidays). Modified (adds local festivals or rest days). Custom (completely different – may isolate the zone from Federation‑wide events).


Education content. Standard (self‑discovery pods – default). Montessori, Waldorf, Classical, Unschooling, or Tracked (academic/vocational streams). Trade‑off: flexibility and creativity vs. rigour and equity. Zones with strong pedagogical philosophies can shape their entire culture around them.


Permanence lock. Off (default – a simple majority can change any rule). On (supermajority plus sunset – protects minority cultures from rapid change but risks stagnation; locks expire after ten years unless renewed).


Narrative Module (optional). Allows a zone to adopt a shared story—fictional, purpose‑game, or historical—with an explicit disclaimer: "This story is a construct. Not objectively true. You may leave anytime." Activation requires a 66% supermajority, 90‑day cooling, citizen jury, and 2‑year sunset. Safeguards: exit fund, no child coercion, no extraction, bench remains, annual review. Prohibited: claims of divine truth, violence, shunning, permanent leadership class. This module allows a zone to build deep collective identity around a mythos without falling into dogma.


Meaning Module (optional). Allows a zone to adopt practices for existential meaning without claiming objective truth. Activation requires a 66–75% supermajority, 90‑day cooling, a citizen jury of eleven, and a 2‑year sunset. Removal is by simple majority with 30‑day cooling. This module is for zones that want to actively cultivate a sense of purpose among residents, not just leave it to private discovery.


Every zone with an active Meaning Module must adhere to six non‑negotiable safeguards. First, the honesty disclaimer: "This framework is a human construction. It is not claimed as objective or universal truth. It works for some people. It may not work for you. You may leave at any time without penalty." Second, an exit fund of at least 5,000 credits per resident for those who leave because the framework no longer fits. Third, no coercion of children: they must receive education about other zones, the bench, and the right to exit at sixteen, and must be exposed to at least two other meaning frameworks before that age. Fourth, no extraction: no donations, tithes, or uncompensated labour beyond the standard work week; no wealth or privilege accumulation by leaders. Fifth, the bench remains in every district; the framework cannot remove, obscure, or claim to answer its question. Sixth, an annual review by a randomly selected panel of five residents audits for coercion, with findings published and the power to recommend suspension.


Permissible frameworks include: Stoic Discipline (morning reflection, evening journaling, voluntary discomfort, text study – the bench is used for evening journaling). Mindfulness and Meditation (silent retreats, guided practice, breath awareness, no supernatural claims – the bench is the primary meditation spot). Craftsmanship (meaning through making with wood, metal, fibre, stone; the bench displays finished pieces for silent contemplation). Service (care roles rotating through elder companionship, childcare, hospice, immigrant mentoring – the bench is for weekly reflection on service). Nature Reverence (daily walks, gardening, seasonal festivals tied to solstices; no claims of divinity – the bench is placed in a garden). Existentialist Practice (no provided meaning; residents gather to discuss that meaning must be created by each individual; the bench is the central monument, and the question Is this enough? is the starting point of every gathering). Ritual Cycle (annual planting, harvest, midwinter silence, and midsummer ceremonies, all optional; the bench is included as a place to sit before and after each ritual). Zones may adopt one framework or a hybrid.


Prohibited are any framework claiming exclusive or divine truth, demanding belief in supernatural entities as a condition of residence, requiring shunning or violence against dissenters, preventing learning about other frameworks, or creating a permanent leadership class with special access to meaning (priests, gurus, guides who cannot be rotated).


The Meaning Module interacts with other optional modules. Under the Education Module, the meaning framework may be taught but the Flaws Curriculum and cross‑zone exposure override exclusivity. Under the Ceremonies Module, supporting rituals are permitted but must be optional and cannot replace the Federation’s Festival of Flaws or Silent Hour. Under the Architectural Style Module, spaces for practice (meditation halls, workshops, gardens) are permitted but the bench must remain in a public, accessible location. Under the Permanence Lock, the module may be locked but the lock expires every ten years and cannot be renewed without another supermajority vote.


Health Module


Options. Public only (default – equitable, free at point of use, but may involve waiting lists). Mixed public‑private (choice and potentially faster access for some, but creates inequality). Prevention‑focused (invests heavily in wellness, diet, and exercise to reduce later disease, but may under‑prioritise acute care). Integrative (includes evidence‑based complementary therapies alongside conventional medicine).


Defense Module


Options. Full contribution (default – the zone pays its assessed share of Federation defense and may maintain a local militia). Reduced (half share, relies more on the Federation; lower cost, slower response to local threats). None (pacifist stance – requires a Federation supermajority to approve, as it effectively free‑rides; the zone cannot field armed force). Enhanced (double share, larger militia, more intensive training – higher security but more expensive and potentially more militarised culture).


Data Privacy Module


Options. Standard (default – Core Protocol rules apply: limited public surveillance, personal data protected). Strict (no surveillance cameras at all, data retention limited to 7 days for any operational need). Maximum (all data is anonymised at source; no pattern analysis on individuals; the closest to the Outpost ideal within a governed zone). Opt‑in transparency (default is no tracking; a user must actively opt in to any data collection beyond the minimum needed for services).


Dispute Resolution Module


Options. Neutral DRO market (default – citizens choose from competing, transparent dispute resolution organizations; accountability through competition). Community circles first (mediation and restorative circles before any formal DRO; builds trust but slower). Zone‑preferred DRO (the zone charter designates a single DRO for all disputes; simpler but risks capture). Restorative only (no punitive DROs; all justice is restorative – humane but may fail for severe crimes where victims demand retribution or incapacitation).


Environmental Module


Options. Standard (default – Federation rules on emissions, waste, and land use). Strict (higher recycling targets, bans on certain materials, mandatory composting). Rewilding (at least 20% of the zone’s land is set aside as no‑human wilderness; lower population density). Experimental (the zone tests new environmental technologies under close monitoring; high risk, high potential reward for the Federation).


Recreation Module


Options. Standard (default – one sports centre per district, parks, and access to The Den). Sports‑focused (more facilities, leagues, and coaching – attracts athletes and active families). Arts‑focused (performance spaces, studios, and galleries – attracts creatives). Nature‑focused (extensive green space, gardens, trails – suits introverts and those seeking quiet). Minimal (only the bench and basic open space; low cost but barren for those who want organised leisure).


Mobility Module


Options. Balanced (default – equal priority for pedestrians, cyclists, and tube pods). Pedestrian priority (car‑free, wide walkways, slow‑speed electric carts only; quieter but slower for goods movement). Tube priority (more stations, faster connections, but more energy‑intensive and expensive). Accessibility‑focused (extra investment in lifts, ramps, and assisted transport; inclusive but costly).


Age Structure Module


Options. Standard (default – legal adulthood at 18, voting at 16, work permitted at 16, Maturity Program completion by 20). Extended adolescence (adulthood at 21, voting at 18, work at 18 – more protection, slower transition to responsibility). Early responsibility (adulthood at 16, voting at 16, work at 14 – faster maturity, but may produce burnout). Elder privileges (voting weight increases with age, elder councils given formal consultative role – values seniority but risks gerontocracy).


Family Policy Module


Options. Standard (default – Federation baseline: 50% parental leave with full stipend, 1.5x grandparent bonus for care). Enhanced leave (75% leave, longer duration – attracts families). Community childcare (shared cooperatives replace much of individual parental care – socialises childrearing). Multi‑generational housing bonus (extra stipend for housing elders with their children – encourages extended families). Reduced benefits (supermajority required; lower benefits to reduce costs, but risky for family formation).


Economic Redistribution Module


Options. None (default – only the Federation’s land value tax and dividend). Local UBI top‑up (an additional 200–1,000 credits per month, funded by zone taxes – reduces poverty, may attract low‑income residents). Progressive income tax (taxes higher merit multipliers or local income; complex but redistributive). Consumption tax (a local sales tax – easy to administer but regressive). Resource rent capture (up to 90% tax on local natural resource extraction; only for resource‑rich zones, turns natural wealth into public wealth).


Exit and Membership Modules


Immigration contract. 24 months (default – filters for genuine commitment, provides time to integrate). 12 months (attracts more immigrants, less filtering, faster path to citizenship). 36 months (strong screen, but may discourage talented people who dislike long trials). None (open borders – anyone may enter and immediately become a resident; welcoming but risks overwhelming social services if too popular).


Exit package (zone top‑up). Minimal (+0, total 15k from the Federation – low cost to the zone, but may trap those who cannot afford to leave). Standard (+15k, total 30k – default, dignified and affordable). Enhanced (+30k, total 45k – a strong guarantee of freedom, but expensive for the zone’s budget).


Outpost access. Full (residents may go to the Outpost and return freely – default). Restricted (may go but not return; one‑way exit). None (no access to the Outpost; the zone is a closed loop). Restricting Outpost access removes the ultimate safety valve but may be desired by zones that want to build a fully immersive culture.


Zone size limit. A numeric cap on population (useful for ecovillages or intimate communities) or none (default – the zone grows as long as people choose it).


Residency tenure for voting. 3 months (default – new arrivals quickly gain a voice). 12 months (a longer threshold ensures voters understand the community before shaping it).


Language proficiency requirement. Off (default – translation technology makes any language functional). Basic (must demonstrate simple Civic Lingua for voting). Full fluency (required for office; preserves a shared tongue but burdens immigrants).


How Numeric Parameters Work


For each numeric parameter—demurrage rate, tax rate, stipend top‑up, contract length—the zone charter defines a current value, a routine adjustment range (min–max), and optionally a step size. Changing within the range follows Type N1: simple majority, 15‑day cooling. Changing the range itself or moving outside it follows Type N2: 66–75% supermajority, 60‑day cooling, citizen jury, and a 2‑year sunset. Moving outside the Federation safety corridor (e.g., a demurrage above 15%, or an exit package below the Federation minimum) requires a Federation‑wide referendum with 60% approval, 90‑day cooling, a Federation jury, and 2‑year sunset. This prevents any single zone from destabilising the Federation while preserving broad autonomy.


Creating a New Zone


A group of at least 50 citizens or 1% of the Federation’s population submits a petition with a draft charter to the Commons Trust. A 30‑day information phase follows, during which the proposal is publicised and discussed. If the charter includes permanence locks or values near the extremes of any safety corridor, a 50‑member deliberative jury convenes to stress‑test the design. After a 30‑day cooling period, committed residents must meet minimum thresholds—200 for a small territory, 0.5% of the Federation for a larger one. A ratification referendum then requires 60% approval by the committed residents. Once the charter is recorded and land allocated, the zone becomes operational when 50% of its committed residents have moved in, or after 180 days, whichever comes first.


Changing an Existing Zone's Rules


Type A (non‑permanent changes): simple majority, 15‑day information, 30‑day deliberation, optional zone jury. Transition support for affected residents—90‑day grace period to move or opt out, extendable to one year. Type B (permanent locks, range changes): supermajority (66–75%), 90‑day cooling, mandatory zone jury, 2‑year sunset. Type C (outside safety corridor): Federation‑wide referendum, 60% approval, 90‑day cooling, Federation jury, 2‑year sunset.


Special Cases: Merging, Splitting, Dissolving


Merge: 66% supermajority in each affected zone, followed by a joint charter drafted by a random committee of citizens from both. Split: 20% of a zone’s residents may propose secession of a territory; the proposal requires a simple majority in both the seceding area and the remaining zone. Dissolve: 75% supermajority; land reverts to the Commons Trust; residents are reassigned to other zones or may take the standard exit package.


How Citizens Choose a Zone


The Zone Directory on every tablet provides maps, a summary of each zone’s module choices, a comparison tool, resident testimonials, and a preview of the exit package one would receive if leaving. Citizens may move freely between zones: they notify the Trust, their housing pod is reassigned, merit scores are recalibrated to the new zone’s system, and any local currency is exchanged. Visitors have full freedom of movement; zones may set reasonable conduct rules but cannot exclude someone based on their zone of origin.


Cross‑Zone Disputes and Jurisdiction


When individuals from different zones have a dispute, they may choose any mutually agreed dispute resolution organization. If they cannot agree, the default is the defendant’s zone DRO. For disputes between zones themselves, a Federation arbitration panel of three DROs is selected by lot, none from the disputing zones. Its ruling is binding. Non‑compliance allows the Commons Trust to withhold infrastructure access.


The Door Is Always Open


The Pluralistic Federation is built on a single insight: no single way of living suits everyone. Human temperaments vary too widely for one constitution, one economy, one culture to serve all. The Merit Zone, Cooperative Commonwealth, Anarchist Federation, Liquid Republic, Sortition Republic, and Outpost each offer a different balance of freedom, community, competition, and care. The Civic Commons serves as the default gateway—the place where immigrants arrive, where citizens may live a simple low‑pressure life, and where the path to the Outpost begins. Beyond these, the module system allows an almost infinite variety of new zones to be born.


Each zone is a choice. Each choice is real because exit is real. The Core Protocol guarantees the floor. The modules allow each zone to shape its own ceiling. And the bench, in every district, facing a wall carved with “Is this enough?”, reminds everyone that no system can provide the final answer. The question is yours alone. The Federation only ensures that when you ask it, you do so without hunger, without fear, and without the distractions of mere survival. That is enough, or it is not, and each person must decide.
FDF Without Utopia Elements
FDF Without Utopia Elements
The Core Protocol — The Only Rules Everyone Must Follow
The Pluralistic Federation begins with a set of rules so basic that almost anyone can accept them. No person or group may initiate force, fraud, or theft against another. Property rights are clearly defined: everyone knows what ownership means, how property can be transferred, and how disputes are resolved. Freedom of movement is absolute—any resident may travel anywhere in the territory except onto private property where the owner says no. Freedom of association means people can form any groups, communities, businesses, or alternative governance structures with whoever wishes to join, and no one may be forced into any association against their will. Freedom of contract allows any voluntary agreement between consenting adults, as long as it does not violate the non‑aggression principle, and those agreements are binding and enforceable. Finally, the right to exit means anyone may leave the territory at any time, taking their property, with no exit taxes, no penalties, and no questions asked. This single right is the master rule that keeps everything else honest: if people can walk away, every zone and every institution must continuously earn their loyalty.


The Commons Trust — A Shared Foundation, Not a Government
At the centre of the federation sits an irrevocable, multi‑jurisdictional trust called the Commons Trust. It holds all shared assets—land, roads, water systems, energy grids—for the benefit of every registered resident. The Trust is deliberately kept weak. It cannot make laws, cannot police anyone, and cannot favour one zone over another. Its sole tasks are to maintain basic infrastructure, collect a land value tax based on the unimproved value of land, distribute an equal monthly cash dividend to every resident, maintain a public list of approved dispute resolution bodies, and update the Core Protocol only by super‑majority vote with independent oversight. The dividend is the same for everyone, regardless of zone, merit, or behaviour. It is calibrated to cover dignified basics—food, shelter, healthcare—but not luxury. It is the floor beneath which no one can fall, and because it can never be taken away, destitution cannot be used as a weapon.


The Zones — Different Ways to Live, Side by Side
Instead of imposing one model of government on everyone, the territory is divided into self‑governing districts called zones. Each zone writes its own internal rules, culture, and economic structure, as long as it obeys the Core Protocol. Residents are free to move between zones at any time, and the ability to leave keeps every zone accountable.


The Merit Zone
For those who thrive on recognition and reward, the Merit Zone adds a voluntary layer on top of the basic dividend. A merit bonus, calculated by rotating citizen review panels, peer feedback, and transparent public records, raises monthly income based on four dimensions: personal development (learning, fitness), social contribution (volunteering, mentoring), responsibility (attendance, promise‑keeping), and adaptability (reskilling, handling change). The bonuses are modest, the floor never moves, and no score is permanent.


The Cooperative Commonwealth
For people who believe that solidarity is a way of living, not just a slogan. Worker cooperatives own and run businesses. Housing cooperatives let residents collectively own their buildings. Neighbourhood assemblies meet regularly to discuss shared concerns, and decisions are reached by consensus whenever possible. Care work—childcare, eldercare, mental health—is organised collectively, not dumped on isolated individuals. There is no formal hierarchy; representation rotates among volunteers.


The Anarchist Federation
For radical individualists who want as little governance as possible. There is no zone‑level government at all. People organise through private contracts, voluntary associations, and free markets. Some enclaves have strong private property norms; others experiment with mutual aid or temporary autonomous zones. The only common thread is the Core Protocol.


The Liquid Republic
For those who want to participate in every decision but cannot be an expert on everything. Every citizen holds a vote on every issue, but they may delegate it to someone they trust on a specific topic. Delegation is instantly revocable, public, and delegates cannot be paid. The delegation graph is transparent, so no hidden influencers can emerge.


The Sortition Republic
For those who believe ordinary people, given good information, govern best. There are no elections and no professional politicians. All decision‑making bodies—assemblies, juries, audit panels—are filled by stratified random lottery from the citizenry. Members serve short, non‑renewable terms and are advised by civil servants who provide expertise but have no vote. This ensures the government looks like the people it serves.


The Civic Commons
A simple default zone for newcomers and those who prefer a quiet life. Residents receive the basic dividend, do a small amount of community work (e.g., ten hours a week of simple tasks), and can stay indefinitely or move to another zone at any time. It is never a dumping ground for the unwanted.


The Merit System — Reward Without Coercion
The merit system is built on a simple premise: extra contribution deserves extra recognition, but no one should ever be threatened with poverty. The basic dividend is untouchable. On top of it, citizens in the Merit Zone can earn a multiplier that increases their monthly income. Assessment is done by people, not machines—rotating panels of fellow citizens, peer reviews, and publicly logged activities like workshop attendance, volunteer hours, or completion of training courses. All metrics are reviewed annually and retired if they become gamed or irrelevant. Merit scores reset every few years, so past glory does not become permanent advantage. And nothing—no multiplier, no prestige, no position—can be passed to children. They start from the same floor as everyone else.


The Economy — Simple, Transparent, and Human‑Paced
The federation’s economy runs on a public ledger that anyone can audit. All public spending, tax records, and infrastructure contracts are visible. Private transactions remain private, but the flow of shared funds is transparent.


The land value tax funds the universal dividend. A small demurrage on idle balances—a tiny, automatic deduction on money that sits untouched for long periods—discourages hoarding and recirculates wealth into public goods. It is not a punishment; it is a gentle nudge toward productive use.


A public‑private accelerator allows citizens to vote on small allocations of shared savings to fund promising innovations. Successful ventures return a share of profits to the public pool; unsuccessful ones are lessons learned, not bailouts. The accelerator is governed by a rotating citizen board, and every decision is public.


Worker cooperatives, consumer cooperatives, sole proprietorships, and small partnerships all operate freely alongside each other. The only limit is the non‑aggression principle. No corporation can lobby for special favours, because public spending is citizen‑controlled and decisions are made in open view.


Justice — Repair, Not Revenge
When a harm occurs, the first step is always a facilitated meeting between the victim and the offender, with a trained mediator. The goal is to agree on a plan to repair the harm—apology, restitution, community service, or rehabilitation. If both parties agree, the matter is settled and recorded.


For serious or contested cases, a randomly selected citizen jury hears evidence, deliberates in public, and decides on the appropriate response. Professional investigators gather facts, but the jury makes the final call. Jury service is a civic duty; members serve short terms and are drawn by lot. No one builds a career as a judge.


A small number of extreme cases—repeated violence, refusal to participate in restoration—may result in confinement. Confinement focuses on rehabilitation and public safety, not punishment for its own sake. Past offence records can be sealed after demonstrable good behaviour, so one mistake does not become a lifelong exclusion.


Civil disputes are handled by competing dispute resolution bodies. People choose any approved mediator or arbitrator they trust. The Commons Trust simply keeps a list of those that meet basic fairness standards.


Education — Learning Without Rankings
Children are not tested, graded, or ranked. Learning happens through immersion, apprenticeship, and self‑directed exploration. Every district provides open‑access study spaces where young people can work on projects, read, build things, or join small group classes. Adults—parents, retirees, skilled volunteers—rotate through as mentors and guides.


There is no fixed curriculum. A child who loves baking spends time at a community bakery; a child fascinated by animals works at a small farm or veterinary clinic. Basic literacy, numeracy, and reasoning are woven into these activities rather than taught in isolation. The goal is mastery at the individual’s own pace.


The Maturity Program is the only mandatory rite of passage. Every young person completes a period of structured difficulty in one of five domains—physical resilience, cognitive challenge, civic leadership, care and service, or creative constraint. They can choose the domain, but they cannot skip the challenge. Completion marks the transition to full adult citizenship.


Health — Care as a Right, Not a Commodity
Every resident has access to basic healthcare—doctor visits, hospital care, medication, mental health support—funded through the Commons dividend and administered by local non‑profit cooperatives. Medical professionals receive fixed salaries, not per‑procedure payments, removing the incentive to over‑treat or under‑treat.


For those who want additional coverage, voluntary mutual aid societies pool contributions and negotiate with providers. There are no for‑profit insurance companies. The focus stays on prevention, community health, and the dignity of the patient.


Anti‑Corruption — Transparency, Not Trust
Every anti‑corruption measure in the federation is low‑tech and structural. The rule is simple: opacity enables extraction, so opacity is systematically removed.


All public budgets, contracts, and policy decisions are recorded on a publicly viewable ledger. The logic behind every vote is available at multiple levels—from a plain‑language summary to the full technical documentation—so any citizen can audit at the depth they choose.


Before any major policy proposal can advance, the proponent must present the strongest opposing argument in writing, verified by a randomly selected review panel. This requirement, called steel‑manning, kills groupthink and forces honest engagement with trade‑offs.


A permanent, publicly funded Opposition institution exists solely to find failures and say so. Its members are chosen by lottery for short, non‑renewable terms, and they are insulated from retaliation. If they identify a problem and the relevant body ignores them for two consecutive review cycles, they can force a binding public referendum. Every finding must include a proposed alternative, so the Opposition cannot collapse into pure criticism.


Citizen juries and assemblies are the engines of accountability. Randomly selected groups of residents hear expert testimony, deliberate, and either approve or block proposals. Their reasoning is published, and their composition rotates constantly.


Sunset clauses are attached to every law, regulation, and spending programme. Unless actively renewed with fresh evidence and a public vote, each one expires. Nothing becomes permanent merely through habit. The same logic applies to performance metrics: if a measure is being gamed or has outlived its usefulness, a citizen review panel retires it.


No single person can move shared funds. Multi‑signature controls require several independent keys held by different individuals, making theft exponentially harder. Leadership roles, from zone council seats to trust protector positions, are temporary by design. Founders of the federation themselves must publicly destroy all special access keys in a witnessed ceremony before any non‑founder arrives, and then live as ordinary citizens with no privileges.


Finally, the right to exit is the ultimate check. If a zone becomes corrupt or oppressive, residents leave—and take their dividend with them. The constant possibility of losing citizens creates an ever‑present pressure to govern well.


How the System Updates Itself
No generation can bind the next. Every twenty‑five years, a randomly selected constitutional convention—composed of ordinary citizens serving a single term—is convened. It reviews the entire framework and may propose a replacement. If a super‑majority of all residents ratifies the new constitution, the old one dissolves. If not, the existing rules continue. This mechanism ensures the foundation is always revisable.


Between conventions, any citizen can propose a change. Proposals gather public support, undergo steel‑manding and jury review, and are put to a vote. The thresholds vary with the scope of the change, but the process is deliberately transparent, slow, and deliberative. Haste is the enemy of legitimacy.


The Outpost — The Door That Proves the System Is Not a Cage
At the edge of the territory lies a permanently unmonitored, ungoverned area called The Outpost. There is no surveillance, no registration, no dividend, and no formal law—only whatever norms the residents choose to build among themselves. People grow food, trade by barter, and govern themselves through a monthly assembly and a rotating council chosen by lottery. A weekly shipment of basic staples arrives from the main city, and in return the Outpost sends back whatever it produces. Only the aggregate weight of goods is recorded at the gate; no individual is ever tracked.


The Outpost exists for a single reason: to prove that the federation is not a total system. Anyone can walk there, become invisible, and never return. Children born there can enter the merit system at sixteen with a clean slate, or stay. The Outpost is the final guarantee that consent is real, because the door is always open both ways.
Protocols
Protocols
Immigration Protocol
Immigration Protocol
Fair Digital Future maintains physically closed borders. Entry is exclusively through a rigorous, transparent, 24‑month program designed to filter for high‑quality immigrants who contribute net value, while blocking opportunists and providing dignified alternatives for those who cannot or choose not to meet merit requirements. The system drives a 15‑20% GDP boost from immigration while keeping the permanent free‑rider rate below 1%.


The Binding Contract
Every applicant – economic migrant, refugee, skilled worker – signs a Day 1 binding contract: “I accept minimum merit rules (60% base: 10 hours/week productive activity plus basic social engagement). After 24 months of support, persistent failure triggers mandatory escalation to assisted exit, contractual eviction, or voluntary transfer to The Outpost.” This contract is legally enforceable under the territory’s sovereign status and sets clear expectations from the start.


Phase 1: Integration (Months 1–6)
Immigrants receive full universal basics (housing, food, healthcare, transport, education) plus intensive personalized support: LinguaGlass instant translation across 220+ languages, a dedicated psychologist, a job coach, and access to self‑discovery education stations. Weekly tablet check‑ins flag barriers – trauma triggers therapy, skill gaps trigger reskilling. Approximately 70% reach the minimum merit threshold during this phase, and they can work and vote (in local matters) from Day 1.


Phase 2: Escalation Support (Months 7–18)
For those who have not yet met the minimum, the system assigns mandatory minimum jobs, tracked by AI and biometrics to prevent faking. Examples: vertical farm harvesting (10h/week, output weighed by sensors), tube station cleaning (8h, RFID tools logged with before/after photos), waste sorting (10h, conveyor volume metrics), or community meal preparation (6h, attendance and portion counts verified). If an immigrant falls below the required threshold, their stipend demurrage ramps to 25% quarterly, and their lifestyle shrinks to plain food, basic hygiene, and no flavored water. The cumulative success rate reaches 85% by the end of this phase. A public dashboard shows real‑time metrics for each immigrant: “$42k invested over 12 months. Job compliance: 62%. Progressing toward citizenship.”


Phase 3: Contractual Exit (Month 19+)
For immigrants who persistently score below 60% merit, the system activates exit procedures. However, the clock resets if genuine effort occurs at any point – inspiration can arrive late. A sustained merit >60% over the final three months (months 21–24) triggers a fresh 12‑month probationary period instead of exit. A maximum of three resets (total five years of cumulative support) is allowed before exit becomes final. This ensures hope without creating an infinite loophole.


The Outpost Option (Added at Month 19)
Immigrants who have demonstrated consistent effort but feel unable to fully commit to the merit system may request transfer to The Outpost instead of pursuing citizenship or exit. The request triggers a 30‑day orientation period: the immigrant visits The Outpost, meets residents, and learns about its governance and way of life. If they choose to proceed, they are transferred with their full work history and cash buffer intact, and the 24‑month clock stops. They may remain in The Outpost indefinitely, doing untracked work with their hands, participating in bingo‑cage governance if they wish, and living completely outside the merit system’s metrics. At any future time, they may re‑enter the merit system by signing the standard citizenship contract and completing a six‑month reintegration period. Their work history (unfakeable metrics) remains portable, and they carry no penalty for their time in The Outpost.


Standard Exit Packages
For those choosing standard exit, a 30‑day voluntary relocation window opens with tailored packages. Economic cases receive $15,000 total ($7,000 entry fee to a host country plus a six‑month stipend covering housing and job placement) to one of 50+ pre‑negotiated labor‑shortage partners – Japan needs farmhands with 500+ logged harvest hours, Gulf states take construction cleaners, Canada fills rural quotas. Their FDF work history and cash buffer make them competitive against unvetted migrants. High‑risk origins (war zones such as Syria or Sudan) trigger a $10,000 UNHCR handover for permanent resettlement to Canada, Australia, or Sweden. UNHCR rules grant third‑country permanent residency with zero pathway back to FDF (biometric lockout and contract termination). No refoulement violation occurs; FDF pays the processing fee.


Phase 4: Sovereign Eviction (Month 25+, <1% of cases)
The rare cases of refusal to comply are handled through a judicial contract breach hearing with individual assessment. UNHCR is notified 30 days prior. A non‑lethal security team escorts the individual via charter flight to their country of origin or a vetted third country, with a minimal kit ($2,000 cash, clothes, hygiene items). No illegal “border dumping” occurs – structured repatriation mirrors Singaporean or UAE visa enforcement. A public dashboard provides transparency: “24 months support = $92k invested. Zero sustained contribution. Eviction preserves resources for 23 future contributors.”


Edge Cases and Human Treatment
